1.title: The famous ‘wrestle’ before God
1.text: Enos calls his all-day, into-the-night prayer a ‘wrestle’ before God—an image of total spiritual effort that has made his account one of the best-loved descriptions of mighty prayer in scripture.

1.template: I went to hunt {0} in the forests

1.question: After Enos prayed all day and into the night, what did the voice tell him?
options: His sins were forgiven and he would be blessed

1.question: Once his own guilt was swept away, for whom did Enos begin to pray?
What did Enos especially desire of the Lord concerning his people?
options: That a record of the Nephites be preserved and one day reach the Lamanites

1.template: Whatsoever thing ye shall ask in {0}, believing that ye shall receive in the name of Christ

pairs.left: His prayer for his own soul

pairs.right: The Lord would visit them by their diligence
A record would be preserved to reach them

1.template: dwelling in {0}, and wandering about in the wilderness

1.title: Farms, flocks, and horses
1.text: Enos pauses his spiritual account to note the Nephites' settled life—tilling the land and raising grain, fruit, cattle, goats, and horses—a glimpse of their economy alongside the wars he records.

1.template: there is a place prepared for you in the {0} of my Father

1.title: ‘A place prepared for you’
1.text: Enos closes not with fear but with joy, certain he will rest with his Redeemer and hear, ‘Come unto me, ye blessed.’ His short book is essentially one long testimony of answered prayer.
